title: Feeding the next generation: Why East Asia’s agricultural support policies need to change
gist: East Asia's decades-old rice-centric agricultural subsidy model — which lifted a generation out of poverty — is now inefficient and regressive; Vietnam's repurposing of 84% of its agricultural support into infrastructure and research doubled agrifood exports, offering a template the Philippines, Indonesia, and Papua New Guinea are now following through the World Bank's Agriconnect and MPA platforms.

themes.theme: The inefficiency of legacy rice-support policy

insights.claim: Rice feeds nearly 690 million people across Southeast Asia; regional production rose from 107 million tons in 1960 to 345 million tons today under decades of subsidized-input, price-controlled, smallholder-rice-centered policy architectures.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Establishes the historical scale and success of the legacy policy model before diagnosing why it is now failing.
insights.locator: Rice feeds nearly 690 million people across Southeast Asia

insights.claim: Globally, agricultural support totals more than US$820 billion annually, but only 35 cents of every dollar reaches farmers in a useful form, indicating that roughly two-thirds of agricultural subsidy spending is misdirected or lost to inefficiency.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A striking efficiency benchmark (35% pass-through) that quantifies the scale of reform upside without requiring new spending.
insights.locator: Globally, agricultural support totals more than US$820 billion annually

insights.claim: In the Philippines and Indonesia, retail rice prices are two to three times higher than world reference prices, a regressive burden on the poor who spend up to a quarter of their food budget on rice, with nearly 230 million people in the region unable to afford a healthy diet.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Legacy rice protectionism functions as a hidden regressive tax on the poor — a specific, measurable policy failure mode for reform advocacy.
insights.locator: In the Philippines and Indonesia, retail rice prices are two to three times

insights.claim: Fertilizer prices spiked 81% in early 2026 while productivity growth in East Asian agriculture is slowing and climate shocks are compressing yields.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A near-term cost shock (81% fertilizer spike) compounds the case for redirecting subsidy spending toward more resilient inputs and infrastructure.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, productivity growth is slowing, fertilizer prices spiked 81%

insights.claim: By 2023, Vietnam had repurposed 84% of its agricultural public support away from distorting subsidies and toward rural infrastructure, research, and extension services.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Vietnam is the clearest quantified case study of large-scale agricultural subsidy repurposing in the region — a benchmark for reform-minded peer governments.
insights.locator: Viet Nam is the most instructive example

insights.claim: Vietnam's agrifood export value more than doubled from US$30 billion in 2015 to US$70 billion in 2025, driven by diversification well beyond rice, coinciding with its subsidy-repurposing reforms.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A direct, quantified growth outcome (export value doubling) tied to the reform template — the strongest evidence point for the article's reform thesis.
insights.locator: Agrifood export value more than doubled

insights.claim: Vietnam is treating the EU's carbon footprint requirements for rice imports not as a trade barrier but as a route to premium market access within the US$1.9 trillion global sustainable food market.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Reframes EU carbon-footprint import requirements as a competitive opportunity rather than a compliance cost — a signal for exporters positioning for EU green-trade rules.
insights.locator: Viet Nam is already treating the EU's carbon footprint requirements

insights.claim: In Vietnam's World Bank-supported Sustainable Agriculture Transformation Project, farmers who adopted sustainable practices cut pesticide use by 48% and increased net profit per hectare by 31.6%, showing no trade-off between environmental outcomes and farmer income.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A concrete win-win data point (pesticide down, profit up) that undercuts the standard assumption of an environment-versus-income trade-off in agricultural reform.
insights.locator: In Viet Nam, the World Bank-supported Sustainable Agriculture Transformation Project

themes.theme: Reform underway in Indonesia and the Philippines

insights.claim: In Indonesia, an $8 million grant supports a pilot moving fertilizer delivery away from centralized subsidies toward soil health cards and cash transfers that put input choice directly in farmers' hands.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A specific, small-scale ($8M) pilot testing decentralized, farmer-choice-based fertilizer delivery as an alternative subsidy model.
insights.locator: In Indonesia, an $8 million grant is supporting a pilot

insights.claim: The Philippines' Rice Tariffication Law of 2019 replaced import restrictions with tariffs, and retail rice prices fell 10–15% within two years; a subsequent $24.5 million grant is now helping redesign the country's fertilizer subsidy into a digital voucher system to improve targeting and cut waste.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A quantified, verifiable prior reform success (10-15% price drop) that supports the broader case for replacing quantitative import restrictions with tariffs.
insights.locator: In the Philippines, the Rice Tariffication Law in 2019

themes.theme: Why reform is now feasible: three converging forces

insights.claim: Fiscal pressure is driving reform as governments can no longer justify large agricultural budgets that fail to deliver affordable food, farmer welfare, and resilience — a shift candidly acknowledged by agriculture ministry officials at a recent Singapore symposium on sustainable agricultural transformation.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Fiscal constraint, not ideology, is the proximate driver of subsidy reform — a signal that reform momentum is likely to persist as long as budgets stay tight.
insights.locator: Three forces have converged to make reform both urgent

insights.claim: Falling technology costs — stress-tolerant seed varieties, precision nutrient management, AI-driven extension, and solar irrigation whose costs have fallen 87% over the past decade — are making higher output per unit of land, water, and public spending achievable at scale.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: The 87% solar-irrigation cost decline is a specific, quantified tailwind lowering the capital cost of East Asian agricultural modernization.

insights.claim: The Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA) for Sustainable Agricultural Transformation is the World Bank's regional lending and knowledge platform for agricultural transformation in East Asia and the Pacific, currently covering the Philippines, Indonesia, and Papua New Guinea.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Names the specific World Bank lending vehicle channeling capital into East Asian agricultural reform — a pipeline to track for related financing deals.
insights.locator: That is the purpose of the Multiphase Programmatic Approach

insights.claim: Agriconnect, a global World Bank Group initiative to transform smallholder farming, create jobs, and strengthen food security, has Papua New Guinea and Vietnam as its first-mover countries in the East Asia and Pacific region.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Identifies Papua New Guinea and Vietnam as the earliest beneficiaries of new WBG smallholder-agriculture financing under Agriconnect.
insights.locator: In the East Asia and Pacific region, Papua New Guinea and Viet Nam

insights.claim: In China's Hubei Province, repurposed public agricultural spending converted chemical-intensive farming into a circular, low-carbon system, establishing 50 eco-farms that produce 220,000 tons of organic fertilizer from agricultural waste with verified soil health improvements.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A quantified circular-agriculture case study (50 eco-farms, 220,000 tons organic fertilizer) offering a replicable model beyond East Asia's rice-focused reforms.
insights.locator: In China's Hubei Province, repurposed public spending
